lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 9 - Attention, Final Projects and LLM Intro

units.name: Machine Translation Evaluation with BLEU
units.description: Introduces automatic evaluation for machine translation, explains BLEU, and situates neural MT historically against phrase-based and syntax-based systems.
units.summary: The segment explains why machine translation needs automatic evaluation and why BLEU became the dominant metric despite known flaws. BLEU scores candidate translations by overlap with one or more human reference translations using n-grams, while also adding a brevity penalty to discourage systems from translating only easy parts [ts=207s] [ts=451s]. The lecturer stresses that translation has many valid outputs, so BLEU can underrate good translations and still award partial credit to bad ones if local words match [ts=355s] [ts=417s]. He then uses BLEU trends to contrast stalled phrase-based and syntax-based MT with the steep improvement of neural MT after 2014–2016, noting modern systems can reach much higher BLEU ranges than earlier systems [ts=520s] [ts=689s].

key_points.text: BLEU evaluates a system translation by n-gram overlap against human reference translations.
BLEU traditionally uses up to 4-gram matches, though the cutoff is a design choice rather than a law.
BLEU includes a brevity penalty so systems cannot score highly by translating only easy fragments.
Translation has many valid outputs, so BLEU can miss good paraphrastic translations.
Neural machine translation rapidly surpassed phrase-based and syntax-based MT after 2015–2016.

units.name: Why Attention Fixes the Seq2Seq Bottleneck
units.description: Motivates attention in encoder-decoder translation and walks through the core decoder-time-step attention procedure before introducing the main equations.
units.summary: This segment motivates attention as a fix for the seq2seq bottleneck where an encoder must compress an entire source sentence into a single hidden state before decoding [ts=793s] [ts=833s]. The lecturer argues that this is implausible for longer sentences and unlike human translation, where people repeatedly look back at the source as they produce target words [ts=856s] [ts=874s]. He then describes the decoding procedure: compare the current decoder state to each encoder state, turn the resulting scores into a softmax distribution over source positions, compute a weighted average of encoder states, and combine that context vector with the decoder hidden state to predict the next word [ts=935s] [ts=1019s]. The discussion closes by formalizing dot-product scoring, softmax-normalized attention weights, and context-vector concatenation before highlighting attention’s performance, interpretability, and gradient-flow benefits [ts=1146s] [ts=1401s].

key_points.text: The original encoder-decoder setup forces all useful source information into one hidden vector.
Attention lets the decoder look back at different source positions as needed during generation.
Attention scores are turned into a softmax distribution over encoder positions.
The attention output is a weighted average of encoder hidden states.
The decoder concatenates the context vector with its hidden state before predicting the next word.
Attention provides interpretability by showing which source words the model focuses on at each decoding step.

units.name: Attention Variants and the Road to Transformers
units.description: Compares dot-product, bilinear/multiplicative, low-rank, and additive attention, then connects these choices to transformer-style projected dot-product attention and positional-information questions.
units.summary: The segment surveys how attention scores can be computed beyond plain dot products. Dot-product attention is simple but crude because an LSTM hidden state stores many kinds of information, not all of which should matter equally for lookup [ts=1556s] [ts=1626s]. Bilinear or multiplicative attention inserts a learned matrix between encoder and decoder states so the model can learn which subspaces should align, even when corresponding information lives in different dimensions [ts=1639s] [ts=1708s]. A low-rank factorization of that matrix leads to projecting vectors into a smaller space before applying dot-product attention, which the lecturer explicitly previews as the transformer pattern [ts=1784s] [ts=1866s]. He also reviews additive attention as the original Bahdanau formulation using a small neural net for score computation, notes the tradeoff between quality and speed, and explains why RNN-based attention did not require extra positional encodings in the same way transformers do [ts=1890s] [ts=2168s].

key_points.text: Plain dot-product attention compares the full encoder and decoder hidden states directly.
Bilinear attention adds a learned matrix so relevant features can align across different dimensions.
Low-rank bilinear attention is equivalent to projecting vectors into a lower-dimensional space and then taking a dot product.
Additive attention computes scores with a small neural network over encoder and decoder states.
In practice, projected dot-product style attention won because it is much simpler and faster.
RNN encoder states already carry order information, so extra positional information was not essential in this setup.

units.name: Final Project Modes, Teams, and Value Add
units.description: Shifts from neural-network content to course project policy, covering collaboration rules, expectations for different team sizes, and the choice between the default and custom final projects.
units.summary: The lecturer transitions from model content to the course final project and emphasizes that using outside code is allowed, but students must document what they used and demonstrate their own value add rather than merely running an existing model [ts=2270s] [ts=2320s]. He frames the project choice as default versus custom, encourages teams of one to three, and explains that larger teams or multi-course/shared efforts are expected to produce proportionally more work [ts=2349s] [ts=2535s]. He also recommends talking with TAs about topic fit and explains the default project: complete and experiment with a minimal BERT implementation for sentiment analysis, then extend it in an open-ended way, for example with paraphrasing, contrastive learning, or LoRA-style adaptation [ts=2606s] [ts=2664s]. The segment contrasts that guided path with custom projects for students who already have a research idea or want the experience of scoping data, methods, and evaluation from scratch [ts=2672s] [ts=2759s].

key_points.text: Using existing code is allowed, but students must document it and contribute clear value add.
Larger teams are expected to deliver proportionally more work than smaller teams.
Shared projects across multiple classes are allowed, but the expected workload scales up accordingly.
The default project centers on finishing and experimenting with a minimal BERT implementation for sentiment analysis.
Custom projects are appropriate when students have a topic they are genuinely excited to investigate.

units.name: Project Constraints, Resources, and Proposal Requirements
units.description: Covers topic restrictions, compute options, LLM API resources, and the required elements of the project proposal and paper review.
units.summary: This segment defines what counts as an acceptable CS224N project and what support students should plan around. Final projects must substantively involve both human language and neural networks, though multimodal combinations are allowed as long as language is central rather than incidental [ts=2770s] [ts=2827s]. The lecturer then reviews realistic compute options—cloud credits, notebook platforms, low-cost GPU services, and Together AI API credits for LLM experiments—while warning that model size strongly affects token budgets and feasibility [ts=2943s] [ts=3155s]. He next explains the project proposal: one per team, centered on both a project plan and a critical two-page research-paper review [ts=3206s] [ts=3260s]. For custom proposals in particular, students must make the data source and evaluation plan concrete, and all proposals now need an ethical-considerations paragraph about deployment risks and mitigations [ts=3308s] [ts=3386s].

key_points.text: A CS224N final project must substantively involve both human language and neural networks.
Cloud and notebook resources are limited, so students should plan projects around realistic compute budgets.
Together AI credits can support experiments on large language models via API access.
The project proposal includes a critical review of a key research paper plus a project plan.
Custom proposals must clearly specify data, evaluation, and an ethical-considerations paragraph.

units.name: Baselines, Milestones, Reports, and Project Idea Patterns
units.description: Explains how projects are evaluated, why baselines matter, what the milestone should demonstrate, and the main families of successful project ideas with concrete examples.
units.summary: The lecturer emphasizes that every project needs an appropriate baseline; without a clear comparison point, it is impossible to tell whether a new method is actually good [ts=3494s] [ts=3554s]. He says the milestone should show that the team has data, code, and at least one runnable system that produces a measurable result, even if it is only a simple baseline [ts=3558s] [ts=3598s]. Final projects are judged largely through the report, which should read like a conference paper with related work, method, data, experiments, analysis, and conclusions [ts=3602s] [ts=3654s]. The segment then catalogs viable project types: task-driven systems, method-driven neural-network modifications, LLM-based system building, interpretability or behavior analysis, and occasional theoretical projects, illustrated with examples like deep poetry, differentiable neural-computer reimplementation, weight tying, quantized models, code-generation for Fortran, and image-to-text fashion cataloging [ts=3690s] [ts=4155s].

key_points.text: Every project should include a baseline that provides a meaningful comparison point.
A milestone should show that the team has a working setup that can run and produce a number.
The final report should be written like a conference paper with method, data, experiments, and analysis.
Successful custom projects can be task-driven, method-driven, interpretability-focused, or theoretical.
Reimplementing an interesting prior paper can be a valid project if it produces meaningful results.
Recent projects increasingly use pretrained LLMs with PEFT or in-context learning rather than training from scratch.

units.name: Pretrained LLMs and Practical Project Design
units.description: Places current project design in the era of large pretrained models, emphasizing practicality, lightweight adaptation, API usage, and research questions that go beyond simply calling a model.
units.summary: The closing segment argues that the research landscape has shifted: earlier projects often tried to invent better architectures from scratch, but most modern work builds on large pretrained models whose architecture is largely fixed [ts=4280s] [ts=4377s]. For most practical projects, the lecturer recommends loading pretrained models—especially via Hugging Face—and adding analysis, adaptation, or surrounding system logic rather than reinventing the base network [ts=4399s] [ts=4428s]. He warns students to stay realistic about scale because many flashy papers rely on compute far beyond course budgets, which is why lightweight methods such as parameter-efficient fine-tuning are attractive [ts=4463s] [ts=4534s]. Finally, he explicitly permits API-based use of models like GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, and LLaMA-class systems, while insisting that a strong project must ask an interesting research question—such as prompting, in-context learning, bias analysis, or reasoning behavior—rather than merely piping documents through a model and stopping there [ts=4550s] [ts=4653s].

key_points.text: Modern projects usually build on large pretrained models rather than inventing new full architectures.
For many practical NLP projects, loading a pretrained model and building analysis or adaptation around it is the sensible default.
Students should prefer practical, lightweight methods such as parameter-efficient fine-tuning under limited compute.
Using commercial or frontier models is acceptable when done through API access.
A project is not complete just because a strong model produced good summaries; the research contribution must come from the surrounding question and analysis.
